skill.name: firecrawl-interact
skill.description: Control a Firecrawl browser session to click, fill forms, navigate JavaScript pages, and extract data after scraping.
skill.summary: Interact with scraped web pages through Firecrawl browser sessions.
skill.author: firecrawl

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, browser storage, and heuristic alerts. Review confirms the skill intentionally documents Firecrawl CLI browser automation and profile persistence, while path traversal, weak crypto, system reconnaissance, and hardcoded URL alerts are documentation false positives. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because it can run browser-session code, access external sites, and persist authenticated browser state.

medium_findings.title: Browser Session Code Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill documents firecrawl interact --code and language options for bash, python, and node. This is intended browser automation, but it can execute powerful actions inside a live browser session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and option table directly show code execution support. The context is legitimate Firecrawl automation, so risk is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Authenticated Browser State
medium_findings.description: The profile workflow persists cookies and localStorage across scrapes and shows login and dashboard examples. This can expose sensitive sessions if users reuse profiles carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly states that profile state includes cookies and localStorage. The behavior is intentional, but it handles sensitive authenticated browser state.

medium_findings.title: External Website Interaction
medium_findings.description: The skill directs users to scrape supplied URLs and then interact with those pages. This creates normal network exposure and must be constrained by user authorization and site policy.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network access is directly shown through firecrawl scrape examples. The examples use placeholder domains, so there is no evidence of malicious exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Alerts Are Markdown Links
low_findings.description: The ../ sequences appear only in relative Markdown links to related Firecrawl skill files. I found no file read, file write, or path traversal logic at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are See also links, not executable code. This makes the static filesystem path traversal alerts very likely false positives.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm and system reconnaissance alerts map to front matter, table labels, or option text. No evidence found of cryptographic code or host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines contain documentation fields and CLI option labels. They do not contain hashing, encryption, uname, whoami, or system inventory behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Placeholder Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs use app.example.com in documentation examples. No evidence found that the skill sends data to a fixed third-party endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains are reserved example domains used in instructions. They do not indicate credential exfiltration or hidden network destinations.

dangerous_patterns.title: firecrawl interact --code
dangerous_patterns.description: The --code option allows precise browser-session actions in bash, python, or node. Review commands before running them, especially on authenticated pages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code execution option is explicit and central to the skill. It is not hidden, but it is a meaningful security-sensitive capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Profile Persistence For Cookies And LocalStorage
dangerous_patterns.description: The --profile workflow saves browser state across sessions. Treat profiles as sensitive because they may contain login cookies and application state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly names cookies and localStorage. The risk depends on user handling of authenticated profiles.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Output Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The -o and --output option accepts a path for output. Users should avoid writing to sensitive locations or overwriting important files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The option table shows an output path parameter. There is no implementation in this skill file, so the overwrite risk is plausible but not proven here.

content.value_statement: Some web data is hidden behind clicks, forms, login flows, or JavaScript state. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code drive Firecrawl browser sessions and extract the needed data.
actual_capabilities: Runs Firecrawl scrape before interactive browser actions.
Uses natural language prompts to click, fill forms, and extract page data.
Uses code-based browser commands for precise interactive control.
Reconnects to prior scrape sessions through saved scrape IDs.
Uses Firecrawl profiles to preserve cookies and localStorage when requested.
Stops interactive sessions to free browser resources.
limitations: Requires the Firecrawl CLI and valid Firecrawl access outside the skill file.
Requires an existing scrape before interact commands can run.
Does not replace Firecrawl search for general web discovery.
Authenticated profiles may contain sensitive browser state and need careful handling.

use_cases.title: Extract Data Behind JavaScript
use_cases.description: Scrape a page, click required controls, wait for dynamic content, and extract tables or details.

use_cases.title: Test Form-Based Flows
use_cases.description: Fill fields, submit forms, and inspect the resulting page state during browser automation checks.

use_cases.title: Review Authenticated Dashboards
use_cases.description: Use a named profile to revisit an authenticated page and extract dashboard information with controlled prompts.

prompt_templates.title: Click And Extract
prompt_templates.prompt: Scrape this page, click the pricing tab, and extract the visible plan names and prices.

prompt_templates.title: Fill A Simple Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Scrape the form page, fill the email field with the test address I provide, submit it, and summarize the result.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Pagination
prompt_templates.prompt: Scrape the catalog page, click through each next page, and collect the item names and availability status.

prompt_templates.title: Use An Authenticated Profile
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the named Firecrawl profile, open the dashboard page, avoid saving changes, and extract the current account metrics.

output_examples.input: Click the pricing section and extract the plans.
output_examples.output: A concise list of plan names, visible prices, billing periods, and any highlighted feature differences.

output_examples.input: Navigate a paginated results list and collect status values.
output_examples.output: A page-by-page summary of each item found, including its displayed status and source page position.

output_examples.input: Use a saved profile to read dashboard values.
output_examples.output: A short report of the visible dashboard metrics, with a note that authenticated profile state was used.

best_practices: Use Firecrawl search or scrape before interact when simple extraction is enough.
Use read-only profile reconnects when you do not need to update browser state.
Stop interactive sessions after extraction to release browser resources.
anti_patterns: Do not use interact for broad web search tasks.
Do not run code-based interactions you have not reviewed.
Do not store or reuse authenticated profiles without approval from the account owner.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code to use Firecrawl for interactive browser scraping after an initial scrape.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it when a page requires clicks, form fills, pagination, login state, or JavaScript interaction before data is visible.

faq.question: Does it perform web search?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly says to use Firecrawl search for web search tasks.

faq.question: Can it work with logged-in pages?
faq.answer: Yes, it documents Firecrawl profiles for saved browser state, including cookies and localStorage.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The main concern is handling authenticated browser profiles and reviewing any code passed to interactive sessions.

faq.question: Does the skill include executable source code?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed package contains one Markdown instruction file with Firecrawl CLI command examples.
